name: Sakaki Branch
description: The Sakaki branch is a sacred evergreen tree branch in Japanese Shinto tradition, considered to be a conduit between the earthly realm and the realm of the kami (spirits or gods). It is often adorned with shide (zigzag paper streamers) and other sacred objects, and used in rituals such as purification ceremonies (oharai) and offerings to the kami. The Sakaki tree itself is revered for its evergreen nature, symbolizing longevity, vitality, and the enduring presence of the divine. In the myth of Amaterasu's hiding in the Ama-no-Iwato (heavenly rock cave), the deities used a Sakaki branch, decorated with jewels, a mirror, and yasakani no magatama (curved jewels), to lure her out. This act is seen as a pivotal moment in Japanese mythology, and the Sakaki branch became a powerful symbol of divine presence, renewal, and the restoration of harmony. Its use in modern Shinto practice reflects its ancient significance as a sacred object that connects humans with the spiritual world, fostering communication and reverence for the kami. The Sakaki branch represents more than just a tree; it encapsulates the essence of Shinto beliefs and the enduring relationship between humanity and the divine.
subtitle: Sacred evergreen conduit to the Kami
origin: Associated with the myth of Amaterasu's return from the Ama-no-Iwato.
currentLocation: Shinto Shrines and homes throughout Japan

keyMyths.title: Amaterasu's Return
keyMyths.description: The deities used a Sakaki branch decorated with jewels, a mirror, and magatama to lure Amaterasu out of the cave, restoring light to the world. The branch served as a focal point for the divine presence and a symbol of hope.

keyMyths.title: Ritual Use
keyMyths.description: Sakaki branches are used in Shinto rituals to purify spaces, make offerings to the kami, and establish a connection between the human and divine realms. They represent respect and reverence for the spirits.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: English translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: English translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another key source for Japanese mythology.
